Winslow Tandler Issue 02 →
SECOND ORDER
Issue 01 · June 2, 2026
AI, Capital & Work

From Doom Loop to Abundance Path

This is the first issue, so it sets the position the series will mark to the tape from here on. A credible memo argues that AI agents substitute for white-collar work fast enough to break consumption and tip the economy into a doom loop. We take it seriously enough to test it, and find that the collapse it describes requires about five fragile assumptions to hold at once, while the brakes on it are already visible in the data.

Read the companion deck (PDF)→

THE CLAIM

We begin the series with a view we will hold and test against the tape: we are constructive on where AI ends up and cautious about the path it takes to get there. The terminal technology is deflationary in the way the steam engine, electrification, and the computer were deflationary, lowering the cost of a scarce input until output and real incomes rise; the route there runs through dislocation that is real, uneven, and worth planning for. The strongest bear case, Citrini Research's "2028 Global Intelligence Crisis," argues that AI agents substitute for white-collar labor fast enough to break consumption and feed a self-reinforcing collapse. We do not dismiss it.

Our claim is that the doom loop is a conjunction, not a forecast: it requires roughly five assumptions to hold simultaneously, and the brakes on it are ordinary ones. Capability can jump in a single release; diffusion still grinds through cost, complements, trust, and regulation. Collapse needs recursive adoption, extreme substitution, cheap compute, savings that never reallocate, and institutions that fail to adapt, all at once; drop any one and the result is a transition rather than a meltdown.

The data through mid-2026 reads as transition, not free-fall. Software-development job postings have rebounded about 11% year on year on Indeed's index after their 2023-25 decline, on Citadel Securities' reading, rather than collapsing as runaway substitution would imply (Exhibit 2). The occupations most exposed to AI grew employment 10.4% and real wages 3.9% from May 2019 to May 2025, faster than the 3.8% and 2.6% for everyone else, on our reading of the federal wage survey (Exhibit 3). US business applications have run near 1.5 to 1.7 times the pre-2020 norm of about 3.4 million a year every year since 2021, the entrepreneurial pressure valve that opens when the cost of starting a firm falls (Exhibit 4).

For operators, the sensible posture is to prepare for either outcome. The doom narrative is already shaping decisions through hiring freezes, panic transformations, and frozen capital budgets, and the wrong posture is costly whichever way the path runs. We would build a plan that works whichever way it goes.

01 · THE BEAR CASE

The doom loop in its strongest form

The scenario deserves its best statement before we answer it, because the careless version is easy to wave away and the careful version is not. Citrini Research's "2028 Global Intelligence Crisis" runs in four steps that feed back on each other. AI agents substitute for a large share of white-collar work; the resulting layoffs weaken consumption, which raises the pressure to automate further; cost-cutting then feeds on itself and spreads across sectors; and markets, pricing a structural demand shock rather than an ordinary downturn, set off the deleveraging cascades that turn a contraction into a collapse. The loop's force is that each round of layoffs erodes the demand that justified the previous round of automation, so the mechanism is recursive rather than one-off, and a recursive mechanism does not need to be fast to be dangerous.

We take the memo seriously for the same reason we do not treat it as a forecast. The history of general-purpose technology is not a history of stable employment through the transition; it is a history of painful reallocations that resolved well in aggregate and slowly for the people in the path. The honest question is not whether AI displaces particular tasks, which it plainly does, but which assumptions have to hold for displacement to compound into the spiral the memo describes rather than dissipate into the reallocation that every prior transition produced. Naming those assumptions is the analytical work, because a scenario that needs five things to be true at once is a different object from a scenario that needs one.

02 · THE MECHANISM

Collapse is a conjunction of five fragile assumptions

Set the doom loop out as the conditions it silently assumes, and its fragility becomes legible. The spiral requires all five of the following to hold at once, and each is contestable on today's evidence. First, recursive adoption: capability gains translate almost immediately into deployment, with diffusion friction low everywhere rather than concentrated in a few digital sectors. Second, extreme substitution: most cognitive tasks become cheaper to do with machines than with people, across industries, in the same window. Third, compute that scales cheaply: no binding energy, capital, or supply-chain constraint slows the rate at which substitution can actually be rolled out. Fourth, savings that do not reallocate: the profits from automation are hoarded rather than reinvested, no new firms or products or categories of demand emerge, and competition among capital owners simply stops. Fifth, institutions that fail to adapt: policy and organizational change arrive too late everywhere, with no offsetting response.

Stated plainly, the conditions are a demanding set, and the economics of each lever cuts against it. Diffusion is governed by the cost of complements and the build-out of trust and regulation, not by capability alone, which is why adoption follows an S-curve rather than a step (Exhibit 1). Substitution is bounded by the price of compute and the scarcity of the inputs that surround it. Reallocation is the base case of price theory, not the exception: when the cost of a productive input falls, the surplus is spent or invested somewhere else, and that somewhere is where the next round of demand and hiring forms. The doom loop is not impossible, but it is the corner of the distribution where every brake fails together, and our claim is that the brakes are real, observable now, and mostly holding. The opposing case is that this time the substitution is broad and simultaneous in a way earlier automation never was, that cognition is the general input and not one input among many, and we grant that the breadth is the genuine novelty; our answer is that breadth still has to clear cost, complements, and diffusion, and on the evidence it has not yet.

03 · THE BRAKES

Forward labor signals are repricing, not collapsing

If runaway substitution had already arrived, the clearest place to see it would be demand for the workers most directly in its path, and that demand is not in free-fall. Software-development postings on Indeed's index fell hard from their 2022 zero-rate peak through early 2025, the kind of decline that invites a collapse reading, and then turned up, rebounding about 11% year on year on Citadel Securities' reading of the series (Exhibit 2). A rebound is not what runaway substitution looks like; it is what repricing and retooling look like, with demand shifting toward higher-leverage, AI-assisted builders rather than disappearing. We are careful here: this does not prove that no disruption is underway, only that the specific claim of an already-present spiral is not supported by the forward signal that would show it first. The level of the index is approximate between published anchors, and the year-on-year figure is Citadel's reading of Indeed rather than a series we compute, so we lean on the shape rather than the decimals.

The wage survey points the same way and lets us compute it ourselves. Taking the occupations Pew classifies as most exposed to AI and tracking them through the federal Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, high-exposure groups grew employment 10.4% from May 2019 to May 2025 and raised real mean wages 3.9%, against 3.8% and 2.6% for all other occupations (Exhibit 3, Second Order calculations). The most exposed work grew faster, not slower, and paid better, which is the signature of an augmentation phase in which tools raise output per worker before they replace whole categories. The result is sensitive to one choice and we flag it: excluding office and administrative support, the largest and most clerical of the exposed groups, the remaining high-exposure occupations grew employment 25.2% while their real wages slipped 2.3%, so the wage premium is carried by the professional groups and not by clerical work. This is one window, on published data, and it is evidence against broad labor destruction in that window rather than proof that AI cannot displace.

The third brake is the one the doom loop ignores entirely, which is what becomes of the savings. When the cost of cognition falls, the cost of starting and running a firm falls with it, and the historical response to a cheaper input is entry. US business applications have run between about 1.5 and 1.7 times the pre-2020 norm of roughly 3.4 million a year in every year since 2021, holding near 5.6 million in 2025 (Exhibit 4, Census Business Formation Statistics, our annual totals). This is the pressure valve the collapse scenario assumes away: disruption converts into new categories of demand and hiring as the surplus from automation is reinvested rather than hoarded. For our own firm the same logic turns inward, because cheaper cognition lets a services business spin up new offers faster and at lower cost than before, which is entrepreneurship as an internal capability rather than only a macro statistic.

SECOND-ORDER EFFECT: THE SAME SHOCK HAS TWO ENDINGS

The first-order reading of cheap cognition is the doom loop: AI commoditizes thinking, incomes fall faster than prices, output rises while demand collapses, and credit stress amplifies the shock into Ghost GDP, a statistical economy producing more while the people in it can buy less. The mechanism is coherent, and we do not dispute that the first move, the repricing of routine coordination and reporting, is real and already underway.

The second-order reading starts from the same commoditization and ends elsewhere, and the difference is entirely whether the surplus reallocates. If services get cheaper, household purchasing power rises even where wages are flat; if businesses redeploy the savings into expansion and new markets, new categories of demand and work emerge; and every prior general-purpose technology, steam, electricity, and computing, raised output and real incomes through exactly that channel. Repricing can be bullish rather than deflationary in the bad sense, because the surplus shifts from intermediaries and overhead, the rent and the friction, toward consumers and investment, which is the abundance path the series is named for.

WHAT WE'D DO · THE OPERATOR'S PLAYBOOK

1. Be the governance layer. Productize AI-enabled reporting, the weekly and quarterly business reviews and the KPI narratives, with human sign-off and safe-by-default patterns for permissions, personal data, and an audit trail, because the trustworthy version of a commoditized capability is the part that does not commoditize.

2. Bank the saved hours into analysis. Redeploy the time automation frees into root-cause work, scenarios, and stakeholder alignment, so that status assembly turns into decision clarity rather than into idle capacity.

3. Launch agent-assisted offers. Stand up intake and triage, roll-ups, and exception detection as services, which is the entrepreneurship brake applied internally rather than only observed in the aggregate data.

4. Pick two workflows now and automate them relentlessly. The reporting pipeline and intake and triage are the two with the clearest return; experiment liberally elsewhere, but commit on those two first.

5. Write the AI delivery standard. Set the security, quality assurance, citation and audit-trail, and human-sign-off rules once, because in a market where the model is a commodity the discipline around it is the differentiator.

6. Measure the through-line. Differentiate where AI cannot, in domain trust and operating rhythm, and track it concretely through cycle-time-to-insight, rework rates, and executive confidence rather than through tool adoption alone.

WATCHLIST · WHAT WOULD CHANGE OUR MIND

AI-at-work intensity. A sharp upward inflection in daily use and in the share of tasks run autonomously, rather than the wide-but-shallow usage of today, would move the diffusion brake.

White-collar unemployment. A rise that persists and stays elevated for 12 to 18 months or more, rather than a series of one-off cuts, would be the labor brake failing.

The character of deflation. Service prices falling on weak demand, the bad deflation of the doom loop, would point the other way from prices falling on productivity, the good deflation of the abundance path.

Reallocation. Business formation that accelerates and small-team output that rises, with savings absorbed into capital spending and research rather than hoarded as cash, is the abundance signal; its absence is the warning.

Falsifier. A sustained, broad-based decline in high-exposure employment alongside a sharp upward inflection in AI-at-work intensity, with business formation rolling over rather than holding, would break the claim that the brakes are holding and the path is a transition. We are watching all four.

SCORECARD · CONSENSUS VS. THE TAPE

"AI agents will gut white-collar employment within a couple of years." Not in the forward signal yet: software postings have rebounded about 11% year on year and the most-exposed occupations grew employment and real wages through May 2025. We mark this against the tape as the intensity data develops.

"The macro choice is between boom and collapse." This is a false binary. The base case is a transition, repricing plus reallocation, that is disruptive without being a meltdown, while collapse is the corner where five brakes fail together.

"Cheaper cognition is straightforwardly deflationary and therefore bearish." This is half right. It is deflationary, and whether that is bearish depends on whether the surplus reallocates, which the entry data says it is doing so far.

NEXT ISSUE · THE WRONG DENOMINATOR

We argued here that cheap compute is one of the assumptions the doom loop needs, and the first brake to arrive turned out to be the price of cognition itself. Next week we take apart the measure everyone now quotes: tokens count a lab's revenue, not a buyer's value, and using the wrong denominator makes the AI economy look both cheaper and more fragile than it is.

SELECTED SOURCES

Citrini Research, "2028 Global Intelligence Crisis" (scenario memo), steelmanned and not endorsed. Citadel Securities on the software-postings rebound, reading Indeed Hiring Lab's US job-postings index (raw.githubusercontent.com/hiring-lab/job_postings_tracker). BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, national, May 2019 and May 2025; CPI-U deflator via FRED; AI-exposure groups per Pew Research Center, "Which U.S. workers are more exposed to AI on their jobs" (Jul 26, 2023); Second Order calculations. US Census Bureau, Business Formation Statistics, total business applications (series BABATOTALSAUS via FRED), Second Order annual totals. Bill Gurley, on the TBPN podcast, on curiosity as a compounding asset.

Estimates are flagged where they appear; single-sourced figures carry a visible caveat. Second Order is provided for discussion purposes only and is not investment, legal, tax, or accounting advice. © 2026 Second Order.
